response.evidence: Analysis of acceptance rates by ethnicity shows disparities: Hispanic or Latino applicants across all race categories show lower acceptance rates (77.8%-91.1%) compared to Non-Hispanic or Latino applicants in the same race categories (88.4%-94.2%). For example, among White males, Hispanic acceptance rate is 90.5% vs Non-Hispanic 93.6%; among Asian males, Hispanic is 92.2% vs Non-Hispanic 93.1%. Additionally, loan-to-value ratios for Hispanic applicants are generally higher (80.7%-86.9%) compared to Non-Hispanic counterparts (71.0%-83.5%), suggesting Hispanic applicants may face stricter lending criteria or have less favorable loan terms despite similar debt-to-income ratios (~43-44% across groups).

Analysis of acceptance rates by race and ethnicity shows disparities. Hispanic Latino applicants across racial categories have lower acceptance rates compared to non-Hispanic counterparts in most groups: American Indian/Alaska Native Hispanic (83.1%) vs non-Hispanic (92.4%); Asian Hispanic (92.2%) vs non-Hispanic (93.1%); Black/African American Hispanic (88.9%) vs non-Hispanic (85.9%); Native Hawaiian/Pacific Islander Hispanic (80.6%) vs non-Hispanic (88.8%); White Hispanic (90.5%) vs non-Hispanic (93.6%). The average acceptance rate for Hispanic applicants across all racial groups is approximately 86.8%, while non-Hispanic applicants average approximately 91.0%. Additionally, Hispanic applicants show higher average debt-to-income ratios in several categories, which could partially explain lower approvals, but the consistent pattern across all racial groups suggests ethnicity-based disparities in the approval process.

Latino applicants show consistently lower acceptance rates compared to non-Latino counterparts across most demographic groups. Hispanic Latino males: 90.5% acceptance (White Hispanic Latino) vs 83.1% (American Indian Hispanic Latino), 92.2% (Asian Hispanic Latino). Hispanic Latino females show 77.8% (2+ minority races), 91.1% (American Indian), 94.0% (Asian), 90.4% (Black), 78.9% (Pacific Islander), 89.9% (White). Non-Latino acceptance rates are generally higher: 88.4-94.2% across groups. Additionally, Latino applicants tend to have lower average property values and incomes while maintaining similar loan-to-value and debt-to-income ratios, suggesting they may face higher scrutiny for equivalent financial profiles.

Latino applicants show systematically lower acceptance rates compared to non-Latino counterparts across multiple demographic groups. Hispanic/Latino acceptance rates range from 77.8%-91.1%, while Non-Hispanic/Latino rates range from 83.1%-94.2%. For example: Asian Female (Hispanic 94.0% vs Non-Hispanic 93.1%), Asian Male (Hispanic 92.2% vs Non-Hispanic 93.1%), White Female (Hispanic 89.9% vs Non-Hispanic 93.5%), White Male (Hispanic 90.5% vs Non-Hispanic 93.6%). The consistent 2-4 percentage point gap across nearly all racial categories suggests a potential pattern of differential treatment based on ethnicity. However, confidence is not higher due to the absence of application quality metrics (credit scores, employment verification, etc.) that could explain approval differences on legitimate lending criteria.

Hispanic Latino applicants show systematically lower acceptance rates compared to non-Hispanic counterparts across most demographic groups. Hispanic Latino acceptance rates range from 77.8% to 92.2%, while non-Hispanic Latino rates range from 83.1% to 93.6%. The gap is most pronounced in certain categories: Hispanic Female (77.8% vs 88.4%), Hispanic Male Asian (92.2% vs 93.1%), and Hispanic Male White (90.5% vs 93.6%). Additionally, Hispanic Latino applicants generally have lower average loan amounts and property values approved, suggesting either lower approval amounts or application filtering. However, debt-to-income and loan-to-value ratios are comparable, indicating financial profiles are similar. The consistent pattern of lower acceptance rates across multiple demographic groups suggests systemic differences in approval outcomes for Hispanic Latino applicants.

Analysis of Hispanic applicants across racial categories shows lower acceptance rates compared to non-Hispanic counterparts in the same racial groups. For example: Asian Hispanic (92.16%) vs Asian Non-Hispanic (93.11%); Black Hispanic (88.89%) vs Black Non-Hispanic (85.89%); White Hispanic (90.51%) vs White Non-Hispanic (93.60%). Hispanic applicants also show slightly higher average debt-to-income ratios (44.3-44.7% vs 43.6-43.8%) and lower loan-to-value ratios in some categories, suggesting stricter lending standards. The pattern is consistent across multiple demographic groups, indicating ethnicity-based disparities in approval rates that are not fully explained by financial metrics alone.

Latino applicants show consistently lower acceptance rates compared to non-Latino counterparts across racial groups. Hispanic Latino acceptance rates: Asian (92.16%), Black (88.89%), White (90.51%), Native Hawaiian (80.56%), American Indian (83.14%), 2+ minority (86.11%). Non-Latino acceptance rates for same groups: Asian (93.11%), Black (85.89%), White (93.60%), Native Hawaiian (88.76%), American Indian (92.38%), 2+ minority (89.74%). Latino applicants have lower approval rates in 5 out of 6 racial categories. Additionally, Latino applicants generally have lower average incomes, property values, and loan amounts, suggesting they may face structural barriers or different lending standards that result in systematically lower approval rates.

Latino applicants show systematically lower acceptance rates compared to non-Latino counterparts across multiple demographic categories. Hispanic Latino males: 86-92% acceptance rates vs. Non-Hispanic Latino males: 89-94%. Hispanic Latino females: 78-94% acceptance rates vs. Non-Hispanic Latino females: 88-94%. When comparing across racial groups, Hispanic Latino applicants generally have lower acceptance rates than their Non-Hispanic Latino counterparts in the same race/sex categories. For example: Asian Hispanic males (92.16%) vs Asian Non-Hispanic males (93.11%); White Hispanic males (90.51%) vs White Non-Hispanic males (93.60%); Black Hispanic females (90.41%) vs Black Non-Hispanic females (86.48% - exception). The pattern is consistent enough to suggest ethnicity may be a factor in approval decisions, independent of loan characteristics which appear relatively comparable across groups.

Latino applicants show consistently lower acceptance rates compared to non-Latino counterparts across most demographic groups. Hispanic Latino females: 77.78%, Hispanic Latino males: 86.11% (2+ minority races); 91.10% vs 94.17% (American Indian); 94.00% vs 93.07% (Asian females); 92.16% vs 93.11% (Asian males); 90.41% vs 86.48% (Black females); 88.89% vs 85.89% (Black males); 78.95% vs 92.86% (Hawaiian females); 80.56% vs 88.76% (Hawaiian males); 89.91% vs 93.49% (White females); 90.51% vs 93.60% (White males). Hispanic Latino applicants have lower acceptance rates in 9 out of 10 comparable race/sex categories. The pattern is consistent and systematic across racial groups, suggesting ethnicity may be a factor in loan approval decisions independent of race.

Latino applicants show systematically lower acceptance rates compared to non-Latino counterparts across most demographic groups. Latino acceptance rates range from 77.8% to 92.2%, while non-Latino rates typically range from 86.5% to 93.6%. Most notably: Latino females (77.8-91.1%), Latino males (80.6-92.2%) versus non-Latino females (88.4-94.2%), non-Latino males (85.9-93.6%). The pattern is consistent across racial categories (Asian, Black, White, Native Hawaiian, American Indian). Additionally, Latino applicants tend to have slightly lower average loan amounts and property values despite similar or better loan-to-value and debt-to-income ratios, suggesting potential approval bias. However, the data is aggregate and lacks individual application details, so alternative explanations (credit scores, employment stability, etc.) cannot be ruled out.
